content: Transformer self-attention computes Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V, where d_k is the key dimension. The sqrt(d_k) scaling prevents dot products from growing too large, which would push softmax into regions with vanishingly small gradients.

Multi-head attention runs h parallel attention heads with different learned projections, then concatenates results: MultiHead(Q,K,V) = Concat(head_1,...,head_h)W^O. Typical configs: GPT-3 uses 96 heads with d_model=12288, giving d_k=128 per head.

Sinusoidal positional encoding uses PE(pos,2i) = sin(pos/10000^(2i/d_model)) and PE(pos,2i+1) = cos(pos/10000^(2i/d_model)). This allows the model to learn relative positions since PE(pos+k) can be expressed as a linear function of PE(pos).

LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) freezes pretrained weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices: W = W_0 + BA where B is d×r and A is r×d. Rank r=4-16 typically captures task-specific adaptations while training only 0.1-1% of parameters.

QLoRA combines 4-bit NormalFloat quantization with LoRA adapters, enabling fine-tuning of 65B parameter models on a single 48GB GPU. It uses double quantization (quantizing the quantization constants) and paged optimizers to manage memory spikes.

Full fine-tuning updates all model parameters and is best when you have >10K high-quality examples and need maximum quality. LoRA/QLoRA is preferred when data is limited (<5K examples), compute is constrained, or you need to serve multiple adapters from one base model.

Naive RAG follows retrieve-then-generate: embed query → search vector DB → inject top-k chunks into prompt → generate. Typical failure modes: retrieves irrelevant chunks, misses relevant ones spread across documents, and can't handle multi-hop reasoning.

Hybrid RAG combines keyword search (BM25/FTS5) with vector semantic search, merging results via reciprocal rank fusion (RRF): score = sum(1/(k+rank_i)). This catches both exact-match terms that embeddings miss and semantic matches that keywords miss.

GraphRAG (Microsoft) extracts entities and relationships into a knowledge graph, applies Leiden community detection for hierarchical clustering, then generates community summaries. This enables 'global search' across entire corpus themes, not just local similarity.

Agentic RAG embeds autonomous agents into retrieval pipelines with reflection, planning, and tool use. Self-reflective RAG evaluates its own retrievals and re-queries when evidence is weak. Corrective RAG validates and corrects outputs before returning.

Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting adds 'Let's think step by step' or provides worked examples, improving accuracy on math/reasoning tasks from ~18% to ~57% (GSM8K benchmark). CoT works best on models >100B parameters; smaller models may produce incoherent chains.

Tree-of-thought (ToT) explores multiple reasoning paths simultaneously, evaluating each branch before committing. On Game of 24, ToT achieves 74% success vs 4% for standard CoT. The key insight is deliberate search with self-evaluation at each step.

Few-shot prompting provides 3-5 input-output examples before the actual query. Order matters: put the most similar example last (recency bias). For classification tasks, balance examples across classes. Zero-shot with good instructions often matches few-shot on capable models.

Self-consistency sampling generates multiple CoT paths (temperature=0.7, n=5-10) and takes the majority vote on the final answer. This improves accuracy by 5-15% over single CoT by marginalizing over different reasoning paths.

HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is the dominant vector search algorithm. It builds a multi-layer graph where higher layers have fewer, more distant connections for coarse navigation, and lower layers have dense local connections for precise search. Typical recall: 95%+ at 1ms latency.

Cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors: cos(a,b) = (a·b)/(||a||*||b||). It's preferred for normalized embeddings (range [-1,1]). Dot product is faster but sensitive to magnitude. Euclidean distance works better for un-normalized vectors.

OpenAI text-embedding-3-small produces 1536-dimensional vectors at $0.02/1M tokens. For cost optimization, you can reduce dimensions to 512 or 256 with minimal quality loss (Matryoshka representation). Sentence-transformers are free alternatives with comparable quality.

GPTQ is a post-training quantization method that uses approximate second-order information to minimize quantization error. It achieves near-lossless INT4 quantization for most models, reducing memory by 4x while maintaining >99% of original accuracy.

AWQ (Activation-Aware Weight Quantization) identifies the 1% of weights that are most important based on activation magnitudes and preserves them at higher precision. This outperforms GPTQ on small models and is faster for inference.

GGUF is the file format for llama.cpp quantized models. It supports Q4_K_M (4-bit, medium quality, best balance), Q5_K_M (5-bit, higher quality), and Q8_0 (8-bit, near-lossless). Q4_K_M is the sweet spot for most use cases: 4x memory reduction with minimal quality loss.

Context window utilization at 40% gives better results than 90%. Strategy: allocate 15% system prompt, 10% agent identity, 25% relevant knowledge, 20% conversation history, 30% working space for reasoning. Trigger compression at 85% utilization.

Perplexity measures how well a model predicts text: PPL = exp(-1/N * sum(log P(token_i))). Lower is better. GPT-4 achieves ~8-10 perplexity on common benchmarks. Perplexity is useful for comparing models on the same test set but not across different datasets.

BLEU score measures n-gram overlap between generated and reference text, with brevity penalty. BLEU-4 uses 1-4 grams. Scores >30 are considered good for translation. ROUGE-L measures longest common subsequence and is preferred for summarization evaluation.

Semantic chunking respects document structure (headers, tables, paragraphs) rather than using fixed-size windows. Optimal chunk size is 256-512 tokens for retrieval. Late chunking embeds entire documents first, then chunks only retrieved documents at query time.

Temperature controls randomness in LLM sampling: T=0 is deterministic (greedy), T=0.3-0.5 is focused but varied, T=0.7-1.0 is creative, T>1.0 is increasingly random. Top-p (nucleus) sampling at p=0.9-0.95 is generally preferred over temperature alone.

KV-cache stores key and value tensors from previous tokens to avoid recomputation during autoregressive generation. Memory usage scales as 2 * n_layers * d_model * seq_len * batch_size * bytes_per_param. For long contexts, KV-cache can exceed model weights in memory.

Mixture of Experts (MoE) activates only a subset of parameters per token. Mixtral 8x7B has 47B total parameters but only activates 13B per token, achieving near-GPT-4 quality at much lower inference cost. Typical: top-2 routing from 8 experts.

Speculative decoding uses a small draft model to generate candidate tokens quickly, then the large model verifies them in a single forward pass. This achieves 2-3x speedup without quality loss, as accepted tokens are identical to what the large model would have generated.

Instruction tuning trains models on (instruction, response) pairs to follow natural language directions. RLHF adds human preference data to align outputs with human values. Constitutional AI (RLAIF) replaces human feedback with AI-generated critiques against principles.

Prompt caching (Anthropic) stores previously seen prompt prefixes and reuses KV-cache. Cached reads cost 0.1x base input price (90% savings). Break-even after just 2 cache hits. Structure prompts with stable prefixes before variable content to maximize hits.

The batch API (both Anthropic and OpenAI) provides 50% cost reduction for non-real-time workloads. Ideal for: background analysis, bulk content generation, agent evaluations, and digest generation. Combined with prompt caching, total savings can reach 95%.

Model routing cascades through providers by cost: Ollama (free local) → Groq (free cloud) → DeepSeek ($0.14/M) → Haiku ($1/M) → Sonnet ($3/M) → Opus ($15/M). This achieves 50-70% cost reduction with zero quality degradation by using expensive models only when needed.

Semantic caching stores LLM responses indexed by embedding similarity, returning cached results for semantically similar queries (cosine > 0.90). This eliminates inference calls on cache hits, reducing costs 20-40% for repetitive workloads like FAQ and analysis patterns.

Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning (RAFT) combines domain-specific fine-tuning with retrieval. The model is trained on documents with both relevant and distractor chunks, learning to identify and use the right context. This outperforms both pure RAG and pure fine-tuning.

ColBERT uses late interaction: queries and documents are encoded independently into token-level embeddings, then matched via MaxSim (maximum similarity per query token). This achieves BERT-quality relevance with 100x faster retrieval than cross-encoders.

LLM hallucination detection: check for internal consistency (does the model contradict itself?), factual grounding (can claims be verified against retrieved context?), and confidence calibration (does P(correct) match actual accuracy?). Semantic entropy across multiple samples is the most reliable automated detector.

Centralized Training Decentralized Execution (CTDE) is the gold standard for multi-agent RL. During training, agents share information for coordination. During execution, each agent acts on local observations only. This enables scalable multi-agent systems without communication overhead at inference.

Mean Field Game Theory models large agent populations (100+) as distributions rather than tracking individuals. Each agent optimizes against the population distribution, reducing O(n^2) interactions to O(n). Essential for systems like ROOT with 172 agents.

The Diversity Prediction Theorem proves collective_error = average_individual_error - prediction_diversity. This mathematically justifies keeping diverse agents (even mediocre ones) in council debates: diversity reduces collective error more than individual accuracy.

Mechanism design (reverse game theory) creates rules so agents' self-interested behavior produces globally optimal outcomes. The VCG mechanism ensures truthful reporting: each agent's cost equals the externality it imposes on others. Applicable to agent task allocation.

DSPy replaces hand-written prompts with declarative signatures (input→output specs) that are automatically optimized. The framework compiles signatures into effective prompts via few-shot example selection and optimization. This removes prompt engineering as a bottleneck.

LangGraph models agent workflows as directed graphs with typed state passing between nodes. Nodes are functions, edges are conditional transitions. Supports checkpointing for durable execution, human-in-the-loop breakpoints, and subgraph composition.

CrewAI separates autonomous collaboration (Crews) from production orchestration (Flows). Crews allow agents to dynamically delegate with role/goal/backstory definitions. Flows add event-driven control, conditional branching, and persistent state management.

Flash Attention computes exact attention in O(n) memory instead of O(n^2) by tiling the computation and avoiding materializing the full attention matrix. Flash Attention 2 achieves 2x speedup over v1 with better parallelism and work partitioning.

Grouped Query Attention (GQA) shares key-value heads across multiple query heads, reducing KV-cache memory by the group ratio. Llama 2 70B uses 8 KV heads shared across 64 query heads (8x reduction). Quality is nearly identical to full multi-head attention.

Sliding window attention limits each token to attend only to the w nearest tokens, reducing attention from O(n^2) to O(n*w). Mistral uses w=4096. Combined with attention sinks (always attend to first few tokens), this handles sequences much longer than the window.

Structured outputs with strict JSON Schema enforcement (OpenAI strict:true, Anthropic tool_use) guarantee model responses match the provided schema exactly. Set additionalProperties:false and mark all fields required. This eliminates parsing errors and retry loops.

Tool use best practices: keep fewer than 20 tools per turn for accuracy. Use deferred loading/tool search when 30+ tools are available. Tool descriptions should include example inputs/outputs. Always validate tool call parameters before execution.

LLM API prices dropped ~80% from 2025 to 2026. Current pricing: Claude Sonnet input $3/M output $15/M, Haiku $0.25/$1.25, Opus $15/$75. GPT-4o $2.50/$10. DeepSeek $0.14/$0.28. These drops make previously expensive agent architectures economically viable.

Parallel tool calling allows the model to request multiple tool calls in a single response. Design tools to be independent (no side effects on each other) for effective parallel execution. This dramatically reduces latency for multi-source data gathering.
